journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
title: Trade-offs between light absorption and energy transfer in a marine light-harvesting complex 2
abstract: Purple bacteria are a diverse group of photosynthetic organisms that can capture and convert light energy with high quantum efficiency across a variety of ecological niches. They absorb light via an array of antenna proteins, primarily light-harvesting complex 2 (LH2), and rapidly transport the energy to the reaction center, where charge separation occurs. LH2 typically consists of eight or nine subunits that each contain three bacteriochlorophyll and one carotenoid. In the marine species Marichromatium (Mch.) purpuratum, the LH2 subunits bind an additional carotenoid, boosting absorbance in the blue where the underwater solar spectrum peaks. In order to accommodate the additional carotenoid, LH2 from Mch. purpuratum consists of only seven subunits, unique among known LH2. Using ultrafast transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy, time-resolved fluorescence, and steady-state techniques, we investigated the effects of these structural differences on the energy transfer dynamics of LH2 from Mch. purpuratum. Our results show, relative to other species, significantly slower rates of energy transfer within LH2 and an excited-state manifold likely to also slow energy transfer between LH2. LH2 from Mch. purpuratum is therefore tuned to match the solar spectrum of its ecological niche, suggesting that the variations in the molecular organization of these antenna proteins may be primarily for optimal light absorption.
discussion: Results and DiscussionB800 and B850 Excited-State Manifold.The position, maxima, and lineshapes provide an initial characterization of the excited state manifold. The absorption spectra for Mch. purpuratum LH2 and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 are shown in Fig. 1B. In the carotenoid region, the well-documented increase in absorbance was observed for Mch. purpuratum LH2 due to the second carotenoid in each subunit . In the BChla Qy region, the prototypical B800 and B850 peaks were observed for Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2. In contrast, the B800 peak appeared only as a shoulder to the substantially larger B850 peak for Mch. purpuratum LH2 due to a reduced extinction coefficient of the B800 band maximum. The wavelength of the B850 band maximum was also blue-shifted to 828 nm similar to low-light variants of LH2 , which is thought to enhance the efficiency of light harvesting for the photosynthetic network .To more clearly resolve both BChla Qy bands, the absorption spectra for Mch. purpuratum LH2 and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 were also collected at 77 K (Fig. 1C) and showed similar features as in previous reports . The B800 peak was significantly broader and shallower for Mch. purpuratum LH2 than for Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2.While the position and amplitudes of the two peaks were observable in both the room temperature and cryogenic absorption spectra, even at cryogenic temperatures there was overlap between the two peaks for Mch. purpuratum LH2, preventing a direct extraction of linewidths. To determine the linewidths, each absorption spectrum was fit to a two-component model representing the B800 and B850 peaks. The arPLS algorithm, a baseline fitting method, was also applied to remove the broad vibronic and excitonic background. The peak profiles were fixed as the empirical profile from Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 for the B850 peak and a Voigt profile for the B800 peak with the maxima and linewidths as fit parameters. The two components extracted from the fit are also plotted in Fig. 1C.The full-width half maxima (FWHM) for the Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 B850 and B800 bands were 258 and 140 cm−1, consistent with previous experimental and theoretical work . The FWHM for Mch. purpuratum LH2 B850 band was 196 cm−1, close to the value for Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2. In contrast, the FWHM for the B800 band was 376 cm−1, which is two- to three-fold larger than for LH2 from other species . Although it is more difficult to fit the room temperature spectrum due to spectral overlap, the extracted FWHM for the B800 band was 510 cm−1. This comparative insensitivity to temperature, resulting in a broad B800 band even at 77 K, suggests that static disorder is the primary broadening mechanism for the B800 band .The larger static disorder in the Mch. purpuratum LH2 B800 band has two potential origins. Both possibilities involve the second carotenoid, and the extended N-terminal domain that binds it, which sits in close proximity to the B800 BChla. First, analysis of the cryo-EM structure revealed both flexibility and heterogeneity in this domain. Three different peptide sequences were also observed in the structures . Consistent with this picture, previous single-molecule studies have shown that heterogeneous polypeptide compositions in LH2 from Rhodopseudomonas palustris can lead to substantial modulations of the BChla energies . Second, electronic interactions between the B800 BChla and the carotenoid may also play a role in the broadening of the band. Studies on LH2 have found fast energy transfer from the carotenoid to the B800 states (0.56 ps for Mch. purpuratum LH2), consistent with their close proximity and suggestive of significant coupling between the states. Notably, the efficiency of this pathway is significantly higher in Mch. purpuratum LH2 than Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2, and those of other previously studied species . Furthermore, the S1 state of the Mch. purpuratum LH2 carotenoid, okenone, has an energy of 12,750 cm−1 , which is nearly isoenergetic with the B800 Qy band and may facilitate mixing between the states. The mobility and heterogeneity of the N-terminal domain to which the carotenoid is bound could then give rise to a large spread in the magnitude of the coupling, potentially broadening the B800 band via state mixing. An analogous mechanism has recently been suggested as a source of broadening of the B850 band due to short-range coupling to optically forbidden charge-transfer states .B800-to-B850 Energy Transfer.In LH2, absorbed energy rapidly relaxes from the B800 to the B850 ring before being transferred to neighboring complexes, and ultimately to the reaction center . To identify the effects of the unique structural features on energy transfer within and between the rings, we performed spectrally resolved transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy at room temperature on Mch. purpuratum LH2 and the well-studied LH2 from Rbl. acidophilus 10050 to compare their dynamics (Fig. 2A and SI Appendix, Fig. S2). Upon initial excitation at 800 nm, a ground-state bleach/stimulated emission signal from the B800 band and the blue edge of the B850 band was present with a maximum at 805 nm (SI Appendix, Fig. S3). The B800 band signal decayed concomitantly with a rise in stimulated emission and excited-state absorption from the B850 band, reflecting B800-to-B850 energy transfer. This energy transfer step is clearly visible in the single-wavelength transients at the peak of the B850 stimulated emission (Fig. 2B). A sharp rise was present in the Mch. purpuratum LH2 data from direct excitation of the B850 band, which was a minor component due to the blue-shift of this band in this LH2. The overall growth of the transient was much slower for Mch. purpuratum LH2 than for Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2, reflecting the slower B800-to-B850 energy transfer. The rates of B800-to-B850 energy transfer were extracted by globally fitting the data across the probe wavelength range using a two-component sequential model . A rate of 1.14 ± 0.11 ps−1 (0.88 ± 0.08 ps) was extracted for B800-to-B850 energy transfer Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2, in line with previous results, and consistent with rates in similar LH2s, whereas for Mch. purpuratum LH2 a much slower rate of 0.36 ± 0.02 ps−1 (2.75±0.15 ps) was extracted. These rates are plotted alongside previously reported rates for other LH2s. Notably, the B800-to-B850 energy transfer rate for Mch. purpuratum LH2 is, to our knowledge, the slowest yet reported.Fig. 2.Transient-absorption spectroscopy and BChla coupling. (A) 2D representation of the TA spectrum of Mch. purpuratum LH2, with stimulated emission peak position indicated via purple dashed line at 835 nm. (B) Comparison of stimulated emission transients from Mch. purpuratum and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2s at 835 nm and 865 nm, respectively. (C) Comparison of B850 band anisotropy from the two LH2s. (D) Overlay of local BChla geometry of the two LH2s with calculated intermolecular couplings from Cupellini et al. . (E) Overlay of the two B850 BChla rings comparing their overall geometry. (F) Comparison of B800-B850 energy transfer rates of four LH2s. Calculated rates based on Förster theory are indicated with ‘x’s.Six panels A to F show a heat map, line graphs of delta A and anisotropy, molecular diagrams, and a rate comparison plot.Context for the unusually slow B800-to-B850 relaxation can be provided in part by the differences in arrangements of BChlas in Mch. purpuratum and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2s (Fig. 2 D and E). The B800 BChlas in Mch. purpuratum LH2 are tilted slightly out of the plane of symmetry and rotated nearly 180 degrees relative to those in Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2, similar to those found in the structure of LH2 from the purple sulfur bacteria Ectothiorhodospira haloalkaliphila . A recent study calculated the electronic couplings for Mch. purpuratum and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 (Fig. 2D) . The geometric differences mean that the largest B800 to B850 BChla couplings were 22 and −22 cm−1 for Mch. purpuratum LH2 and −8 and 38 cm−1 for Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2.To understand the effects of these couplings, we calculated the rate of B800-to-B850 energy transfer for both complexes using generalized Förster theory . The calculated B800-to-B850 energy transfer rates were 0.40 ps−1 and 0.50 ps−1 for Mch. purpuratum and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2, respectively (SI Appendix, Fig. S6A). In the calculations, the rate in Mch. purpuratum LH2 is reduced due to a decrease in electronic coupling from the change in orientation of the B800 BChla (Fig. 2D). While the calculated values are in agreement with the experimental trend, they fail to reproduce the dramatic threefold difference we observed in our TA spectroscopy.There are multiple other factors that can influence the rate of energy transfer in Mch. purpuratum LH2. The unusual properties of the B800 band provide the most likely explanations for the slow B800-to-B850 energy transfer. First, the static disorder that broadens the B800 band also affects energy transfer. Although static disorder is known in some cases to increase the rate of B800-to-B850 energy transfer due to increased spectral overlap , further broadening of the B800 band can decrease this overlap and thus the rate of energy transfer (SI Appendix, Fig. S7). Supporting this picture, the species-associated spectra extracted from global fitting show only a very weak B800 ground-state bleach for Mch. purpuratum LH2 (SI Appendix, Fig. S3). This suggests either a substantial broadening or a reduction in the oscillator strength of the B800 band as compared to other LH2 variants , in agreement with the fitting of the cryogenic absorption spectra (Fig. 1C). Second, a reduction in oscillator strength, possibly due to a change in the local polarizability of the binding pocket, could contribute to the slow B800-to-B850 energy transfer which, in the generalized Förster theory model, scales with the oscillator strength of the BChlas in both bands . Finally, the S1 state of okenone is energetically close enough to the B800 band to allow reverse energy transfer; at room temperature, the energy gap of 250 cm−1 implies a reverse rate of 0.54 ps−1 (1.85 ps), large enough to provide kinetic competition to B800-to-B850 relaxation and potentially reduce this energy transfer rate. These characteristics of the B800 band suggest a unique role in Mch. purpuratum, in which its light-harvesting capabilities in the IR are de-emphasized, consistent with the reduced solar intensity in this region (Fig. 1B). Instead, it may simply serve as a conduit, transferring energy from the neighboring second carotenoid to the B850 band.B850 Relaxation.To characterize energy relaxation within each band, we also performed TA anisotropy studies, which can resolve transfer between spectrally overlapping transitions. TA anisotropy measures the orientation of the transition dipole relative to the initial excitation, and its decay reflects energy migration around the ring. The TA anisotropy transients for the B850 bands of both Mch. purpuratum LH2 and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 are shown in Fig. 2C. Both transients were well fit with a monoexponential function. A timescale of 104 ± 10 fs was extracted for Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2, in agreement with previous findings for this complex and similar LH2s . However, a timescale of 203 ± 13 fs was extracted for Mch. purpuratum LH2, once again reflecting substantially slower dynamics than in other LH2s. Consistent with these timescales, couplings within the B850 ring were smaller for Mch. purpuratum LH2 than for Rbl. acidophilus LH2 at 166 cm−1 and 298 cm−1, respectively , due to steeper angles and larger separations between the BChla on adjacent subunits. As shown in SI Appendix, Fig. S1, the lower symmetry of Mch. purpuratum LH2 positions the pair of B850 BChla from each subunit approximately 2 Å further away from the pair on the adjacent subunits, with steeper pairwise angles between them as compared to Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 . In a simple Förster theory model with a square dependence of the energy transfer rate on the intramolecular coupling , these coupling values provide approximate agreement with the relative rates of energy relaxation around the B850 ring from the anisotropy decay.The TA anisotropy transient for the B800 band of Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 is shown in SI Appendix, Fig. S5C. The transient was also well fit with monoexponential function. A timescale of 860 ± 40 fs was extracted, again in agreement with previous findings for this complex and similar LH2s . However, the anisotropy decay of the B800 band of Mch. purpuratum LH2 was hidden by the grow-in of the overlapping spectral signal of the B850 excited-state absorption feature, as was previously observed for LH3 . Notably, in contrast to previous observations for LH3, the relative perpendicular polarization response of the B800 band was a third of the corresponding value typical of LH2s (SI Appendix, Fig. S5A), even at short time delays. The smaller anisotropy suggests minimal intraband B800 relaxation. Consistently, there is weaker coupling between the B800 BChla in Mch. purpuratum than in Rbl. acidophilus at 8 cm−1 and 30 cm−1, respectively, which can be attributed to a 3 Å larger separation in Mch. purpuratum.Superradiance.In addition to the reduction in energy transfer rates, the changes in BChla geometry due to the heptameric organization of LH2 from Mch. purpuratum also reduce the degree of delocalization, which influences energy transfer within and between LH2. In delocalized states, coupling extends the coherence of an excitation across multiple chromophores. The delocalization of the B850 band, induced by the strong coupling within this ring, is in large part responsible for the substantial redshift of its transitions. The low-energy excitonic states of this band are primarily responsible for transporting energy through the network of LH2 complexes . For the BChla arrangement of the B850 ring, one characteristic of its delocalized states is superradiance, in which the radiative emission is enhanced over that of an excitation on an individual BChla . Quantification of the superradiance provides a metric for the number of BChla over which the delocalized state extends . The superradiance is calculated from the radiative rate of LH2 as compared to isolated BChla, and the radiative rate is determined from the ratio of the fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield .To determine how superradiance depends on LH2 oligomerization, we measured the fluorescence lifetimes (Fig. 3A) and quantum yields (Fig. 3B) of the LH2 complexes of Mch. purpuratum, Rbl. acidophilus 10050, and Phaeospirillum (Phs.) molischianum. From the fluorescence lifetimes and quantum yields, the relative radiative rates were calculated and compared to previously reported values for the model species Rhodobacter (Rba.) sphaeroides . In Fig. 3C, the radiative rates are plotted per BChla, where values greater than one indicate superradiance. The degree of superradiance observed in Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 and Rba. sphaeroides LH2 are similar, consistent with their common nonameric geometry. Both the heptamer, Mch. purpuratum LH2, and the octamer, Phs. molischianum LH2, exhibited lower radiative rates than the two nonamers. The smaller delocalization lengths implied by these values agrees with our observations of slower energy relaxation in Mch. purpuratum LH2 and similar observations previously reported for Phs. molischianum LH2 . These differences in radiative rates for the lower-symmetry LH2s can be explained by decreased coupling in their B850 bands due to increased separations and larger angles between B850 BChla on adjacent subunits. The especially low radiative rate of Phs. molischianum LH2 may also be related to the relatively large static disorder of its B850 band . These findings show that the symmetry of the complex and the degree of intraband B850 coupling can have a substantial impact on exciton delocalization in LH2.Fig. 3.B850 anisotropy and coupling. (A) Time-resolved fluorescence decays of LH2 from four species recorded by time-correlated single photon counting. (B) Relative quantum yield plot of the four different complexes. The relative quantum yield is given by the ratio of the total photons emitted over the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, which can be extracted from the zero-intercept fits to data taken at multiple concentrations (dashed lines). (C) Fluorescence lifetimes, quantum yields, and effective radiative rates of LH2 from four species as a function of the number of subunits.Three-panel figure with A: intensity decay curves, B: photon flux vs absorbance lines, and C: scatter plots of fluorescence data.Overall energy transport and trapping in photosynthetic membranes is a multistep process, involving transfer between LH2s, to LH1, and ultimately to the RC on timescales of 3 to 60 ps . In many organisms and growth conditions, the formation of arrays of LH2 means that multiple LH2-to-LH2 steps are required . Reducing the rate of this step could, therefore, impact the timescales of transport and trapping. LH2-to-LH2 energy transfer depends on both the properties of LH2 and their organization in the membrane. In terms of LH2 properties, delocalization of the lowest-lying excitonic states of the B850 band is thought to enhance energy transfer between LH2 due to their increased oscillator strength . The decreased delocalization length associated with lower symmetry is likely to, therefore, slow energy transfer between these LH2s. In terms of LH2 organization, previous research has suggested that optimal packing geometries are achieved with octameric and nonameric LH2. Thus, the packing geometry generated by heptameric LH2 may also slow energy transfer between LH2 . Both LH2 properties and organization, therefore, point to slower energy transport in Mch. purpuratum, although in vivo the complexity of the system can give rise to unexpected effects.
conclusion: ConclusionIn this work, we report the excited states and energy relaxation within LH2 from the purple sulfur bacteria Mch. purpuratum. Our results show that the inclusion of an additional carotenoid to enhance underwater light absorption leads to dramatic consequences for energy transfer within LH2, slowing relaxation steps two- to threefold. The carotenoid imposes a symmetry reduction on LH2 that also decreases exciton delocalization, long thought to be a hallmark of the function of this complex. Extensive previous research on LH2 from purple nonsulfur bacteria focused on the ways in which it is optimized for rapid energy transfer within the complex; our results show that the changes to the electronic structure of Mch. purpuratum LH2 enhance underwater light absorption while slowing energy transfer. This adaptation suggests that, at least in certain environments, the absorption profile may be a more important adaptation criterion than the energy transfer rates within LH2, which may even simply be a byproduct of the high BChla densities required for photon capture. Indeed, although the energy transfer rates we have reported for Mch. purpuratum LH2 are substantially slower than those found in the LH2 of other species, they are still much faster than the energy transfer steps between complexes, including LH1, the reaction center, and other LH2s, that limit the overall rates for energy transport and conversion.Our results also highlight the flexibility of the functional roles played by carotenoids and BChla. In the marine habitats of Mch. purpuratum, in which near-IR light is relatively unavailable, carotenoids become critical for light absorption. In the presence of the additional carotenoid, the role of the B800 BChla in Mch. purpuratum LH2 is altered: they serve primarily as a conduit from the carotenoid to the B850 band. Notably, this conduit appears to be robust to accompanying changes in excited-state properties of the B800 BChla. As observed here, the differences in light absorption and their effect on energy transfer underscore the flexibility of LH2 and its versatility across environments. Further investigation of other LH2s from underexplored taxa can yield functional and foundational insights into solar energy systems.
figure_legends: Structures and absorption spectra of Mch. purpuratum and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2s. (A) Structures of Mch. purpuratum LH2 and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2. The Top panel shows a periplasmic view of the complexes, and the Bottom panel shows a ribbon structure of individual subunits with bacteriochlorophylls (red) and carotenoids (teal). (B) Absorption spectra of the two complexes, in units of molar absorptivity per subunit. Overlaid are solar irradiance spectra near the surface (orange) and screened by 2 m of water (purple), color-coded to more typical niches for the two species. (C) Mch. purpuratum LH2 and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2 spectra at 77 K, with fitted backgrounds removed, room-temperature spectra overlaid, and fits to B850 (dashed) and B800 (dotted) bands indicated.

Transient-absorption spectroscopy and BChla coupling. (B) Comparison of stimulated emission transients from Mch. purpuratum and Rbl. acidophilus 10050 LH2s at 835 nm and 865 nm, respectively. (C) Comparison of B850 band anisotropy from the two LH2s. (D) Overlay of local BChla geometry of the two LH2s with calculated intermolecular couplings from Cupellini et al. . (E) Overlay of the two B850 BChla rings comparing their overall geometry. (F) Comparison of B800-B850 energy transfer rates of four LH2s. Calculated rates based on Förster theory are indicated with ‘x’s.

B850 anisotropy and coupling. (A) Time-resolved fluorescence decays of LH2 from four species recorded by time-correlated single photon counting.
